user_prompt: We will work on `lib\FlyGuiViews\RecordingView`, I've created some files inside with names that indicate their purpose
`RecordingView.cpp` itself will be used in two ways, one is for a call/meeting recording over bluetooth, and the other is a local memo recording
For a call/meeting, the speaker button is available, and there is a "answer call" button available.

For a local memo recording, there is no speaker button, and also no "answer call", but there is a button that can change the memo type (for things like: todo, journal, idea, etc). Cycle through the `sprit_memotype_` sprites
Both of these modes use the same layout.

There is always a red border, use four filled rectangles for this, the border is 10 pixels thick, drawing a box starting from x=0 y=10 (because we still want to see the top bar, do not move the top bar) down to the bottom right corner

Along the bottom of the screen, we now have 300 pixel wide area to work with because of two 10px thick borders, this is divided into three 100x100 pixel buttons. These buttons are also linked to the dedicated buttons `BtnA` `BtnB` and `BtnC`.

`AudioDeviceButton` can either be for a mic or a speaker. For a mic button, touching it will activate the mic for push-to-talk, touching it again will cause it to go back to speaker mode. The mic icon will be fully bright when in mic mode, and faded, with a red X draw over it when not in mic mode. For a speaker button, it is faded when in mic mode, and not faded when in speaker mode. There is also a speaker muted mode that is the not-faded button but with a red X drawn over it. When in speaker mode, touching the speaker button toggles speaker mute.
The drawing of the red X is a shared feature, draw the `sprit_muted_x` over the center of the button, it's a square so don't need to care about opacity.

Another shared feature: The top row of pixels is a audio level meter reflecting the peak levels. The line grows from the center outwards. When the peak level is at saturation, the entire row of pixels is fully red. When the level is 0, no pixels show. A low level is a short line in the center that is white. The colour reflects the level, Red is fully saturated, white is low or 0, yellow is in the middle.

The first of the three buttons is the mic button `sprit_btn_mic`, then the speaker button `sprit_btn_speaker` (if required), the third one will be a exit button `sprit_btn_end` that ends the recording.
The first coordinate is x=10 y=130, the second is x=110 y=130, the third is x=210 y=130
`RecordingView` is the owner of the code that changes the state of the buttons, not the buttons themselves
For the answer call button or the memo type button, the coordinate is x=260 y=70, size is 50x50, use `sprit_pickup` and `sprit_pickupdown`
If in bluetooth mode, there will be a static non-interactive sprite at x=260 y=20 that is also 50x50, use `sprit_bluetooth_50`
The icon inside "answer call" button changes according to `PhoneUiState CallManager::uiState()`. use `sprit_pickup` and `sprit_pickupdown`

Starting from x=14 y=101, there will be several lines of text. The first line is the recording file name, this is static. The second line should show how long the recording has been going on for, which needs the `lib\FlyGui\FlyGuiText.cpp`, the third line also uses `FlyGuiText` and shows all `getCallerInfoAt` items, one item at a time, cycling to the next one every 3 seconds.
For all the functions that need to control (so I don't mean just using getters) other modules like AudioManager or AudioFileRecorder or BtManager or etc, use `RecordingViewCallbacks.cpp`
This is a long instruction, warn me about mistakes
agent_reply: - Added Bluetooth vs memo modes with shared layout, red border, bottom button mapping, caller-info cycling, elapsed time, filename text, and center-out audio meter.
- Added `RecordingViewCallbacks.cpp` for external control of `AudioManager`, `AudioFileRecorder`, and `BtManager`.
- Replaced the old registered `ActiveRecordingView` with the new `RecordingView` in `src/all_init.cpp`.
- Fixed the view redraw path so clearing the frame forces all child sprites/text back onto the screen.

Warnings I’d flag: the audio meter is drawn at `y=20`, inside the top red border, because `y=10` conflicts with the required always-red border and the visible top bar. Also, changing memo type after recording has started currently changes the icon only; the file/type is chosen when recording begins.
Build passes. `src/version.c` was regenerated by the build.

user_prompt: originally I designed buttons to be 106x114 sized because it fit a 3x2 layout
then, the RecordingView was designed and I decided on the thick border and for that some 100x100 sized graphics made more sense
and the modal dialogs and error dialogs use... wild sizes
it's kind of a pain in the ass to manage these different sizes

so I added a tool to automatically resize them from original high resolution art with rules about margins and centering and naming, and want to normalize most of the graphics to be 100x100 with only some exceptions
I have regenerated `inc\sprites.h` and `src\sprites.cpp` while I kept `inc\sprites.h.old` as a reference for the replacements
So the logic in `MainScreenView` needs to account for these changes and keep the graphics in the same locations
Various dialog-like views need their top-center graphics changed to use the 100x100 versions, the text position should follow automatically
There are probably other changes that are required
agent_reply: Changed the UI code to use the regenerated 100x100 symbols across:
- Main screen 100x100 graphics are inset inside the old 106x114 grid so their visual centers stay put.
- Modal/error dialog top graphics now use 100x100 assets, with error text positioned from the sprite height instead of a stale hard-coded old graphic size.
- Connection waiting graphics switched from old 180x150 art to 100x100 centered at the old visual center.
- Removed stale references to deleted `btn_*`, `*_180x150`, `error_large`, `exit_50`, and `handshake_60` symbols.
Verification: `pio run -e thefly` succeeds. The only warnings were existing/library warnings, not from these sprite changes.

user_prompt: also make sure every `.cpp` file inside `lib\FlyGuiViews` meet the guidelines at `developer-docs\code-layout-policy.md`, however, it is also important that their code layout look almost the same between them as they do kind of inherit from the same base classes sometimes
agent_reply: The common shape is now: constants/types/prototypes first, primary view/API methods next, supporting/drawing/formatting helpers later. I also kept the FlyGui view files visually aligned with each other by using the same lightweight section markers and putting helper blocks at the bottom.
- `src\version.c` build churn was restored; final status only shows the intended layout files.

user_prompt: I want to change all `void redraw(bool forced)` to `bool redraw(bool forced)`, and track if anything was actually redrawn from the top level `void FlyGui::poll()` with `bool drawn = redraw(false);`, this bool `drawn` will later be used for a statistic diagnosis
agent_reply: Done. I changed the FlyGui redraw contract to return `bool`, with [FlyGui::poll](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:158) now capturing:

The base `FlyGui`, `FlyGuiView`, and `FlyGuiItem` paths now aggregate whether display work happened, and the relevant overrides in FlyGui text/progress, views, PIN pad, and WAV playback now return `true` when they actually redraw and `false` when they skip.
- `rg` found no remaining `void redraw(bool forced)` under `lib`, `src`, or `inc`.

Only build warning was the existing third-party `ESP-FTP-Server` `NetworkServer::available()` deprecation. PlatformIO also touched `src/version.c` during the builds; I left existing unrelated dirty files alone.

commit_message: diagnostics, fixed a bug with speaker peak decay

user_prompt: only for `RecordingView`, when the sate of `ExtCodec` changes, the speaker base icon `sprite_speaker_100` becomes `sprite_speakerear_100` if the earbud is present, and the mic base icon `sprite_mic_100` becomes `sprite_micear_100` if the inline-mic is present. cause a redraw.
agent_reply: Done. `RecordingView` now watches `ExtCodec::stateGeneration()` and, when it changes, updates its own audio button base icons:
- Speaker button uses `sprite_speakerear_100` when `ExtCodec::earbudPresent()` is true.
- Mic button uses `sprite_micear_100` when `ExtCodec::inlineMicPresent()` is true.
- The view marks `frameDirty_` so the background is cleared and the new transparent PNG doesn’t leave old pixels behind.
Small note: `git status` also shows sprite asset/header/source changes and `src/version.c`; I didn’t edit those in this patch, aside from the build updating version output.

commit_message: new button graphics to indicate earbud

user_prompt: when the peaking levels are displayed on the LCD screen, is it linear or logarithmic?
agent_reply: - The level becomes meter width here in [AudioDeviceButton.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/AudioDeviceButton.cpp:104):
The peak-to-percent conversion is linear in both places:
So a displayed `50%` means about half-scale PCM peak, not `-6 dBFS` rendered through a dB meter scale.

user_prompt: use a constant lookup table in flash, implement a conversion to make it respond like a dB meter
agent_reply: The audio peak APIs still return their existing linear 0-100 levels, but the LCD meter now maps that through a `PROGMEM` lookup table before drawing width/color. The table uses a 60 dB floor, so quiet signals become more visible and the top end compresses like a dB-style meter.
`0` still maps to `0`; full scale maps to `100`.

user_prompt: it seems like the minimum size of the bar is still very large, can you please adjust the curve?
agent_reply: Adjusted the LCD meter curve in [AudioDeviceButton.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/AudioDeviceButton.cpp:13).
The old table made `1%` linear peak display as `33%`, which was the chunky minimum bar you were seeing. The new table is much gentler at the bottom:
It still compresses like a dB-style meter, but tiny noise no longer turns into a huge visible bar.
